chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q1_analysis: The paper explicitly mentions that the artifact (ReCraft code and Rocq proof sketches) is available at a public Zenodo link: https://zenodo.org/records/15283088. This meets the criteria for 2 points.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q2_analysis: The evaluation was performed by implementing ReCraft in etcd (a real system) and evaluating it on a 'public research cloud' using VM instances. Furthermore, they compared it against an emulation of TiKV/CockroachDB. The use of a public cloud environment with VMs constitutes a high-fidelity, large-scale simulation/deployment setup, qualifying for 2 points.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q3_analysis: The paper has a very clear and dedicated section (Section III-A) detailing the 'Failure Assumptions' and explicitly defining the failure model: 'it assumes an asynchronous network and non-Byzantine node failures.' It also specifies tolerance limits ($f = n - q$). This is a formal definition, warranting 2 points.
chain_of_thought_evaluation.Q4_analysis: The paper provides rigorous, data-driven analysis of trade-offs. Section VII-A compares write performance against unmodified etcd. Sections VII-B and VII-C provide latency and throughput comparisons between ReCraft and TC (the emulated baseline), quantifying the overheads (e.g., 'TC takes on average 21x longer'). This meets the criteria for 2 points.

major_weakness: The paper is exceptionally strong across all metrics; the only minor weakness is that the formal proofs are referenced in a technical report rather than fully presented within the main text.
